1	# Auxilio Workspace
2	
3	Google **Workspace Add-on** for Auxilio (Office Ops). Lets a host register an in-person visit **from inside the Google Calendar event** — the Google-sanctioned, mobile-capable version of the "Visit tab" idea. This is a **channel surface**, not a backend: it renders cards and forwards intent to `office-ops-engine`.
4	
5	> Read the umbrella `../CLAUDE.md` for the four constraints and UX rules. They apply here too.
6	
7	## What this is (and isn't)
8	
9	- **Is:** an Apps Script add-on (CardService) in the Calendar right rail on **web + the mobile Calendar app**. **The whole host flow lives in the card** (card-first, D5) — roster, per-guest include toggle, name/phone edits, template + location pickers, email preview, send, manage — via card form inputs + action handlers that call the engine over HTTPS.
10	- **Isn't:** a place for business logic. Dedup, extraction, invite/pass issuance, and lifecycle flows all live in `office-ops-engine`'s visitor plugin. This repo is a **dumb adapter** (per the umbrella architecture).
11	- **Isn't (v1):** a web app. We **do not** bounce the host to a browser tab — that breaks "everything in front of the user," worst on mobile. A full web editor is **deferred/optional** (D6), built only on evidence the card ceiling is real.
12	
13	## Hard rules (decided 2026-06-16)
14	
15	1. **Contract lives in the engine, not here.** The add-on POSTs to `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/register` with a Google ID token; the engine verifies it, normalizes to a `NormalizedCalendarEvent{ source: 'marketplace' }`, and feeds the existing spine `ingestCalendarEvent` — the same one the `.ics`/magic-address ingest calls. Dedup is on `tenantId + iCalUID` (the `calendar_event_links` ledger), so the add-on path and the room-resource path converge instead of duplicating. **Send the canonical `iCalUID`** (from the Advanced Calendar Service `Calendar.Events.get(...).iCalUID`) so convergence actually works. Full contract: `../office-ops-engine/spec/2026-06-16_calendar_addon_register_endpoint.md`.
16	2. **Internal-first.** Published privately to the Appointy Workspace domain (admin one-click push to all users — no per-user install). **No Google OAuth verification needed while internal.** Public Marketplace listing is deferred packaging over this same code.
17	3. **Two-way without broad OAuth.** Use the narrow current-event scopes:
18	   - `addOns.calendar.currentEventAccess = READ_WRITE`
19	   - `.../auth/calendar.addons.current.event.read`, `.../auth/calendar.addons.current.event.write`
20	
21	   Attendees arrive in the trigger payload (no Calendar API call). `READ_WRITE` lets us write the pass link / "✓ Registered" back onto the *open* event. Broad calendar OAuth stays in the engine's ingest pipeline — **do not duplicate it here.**
22	4. **Calendar-only scopes. Never add Gmail or Drive.** Calendar is a *sensitive* scope (verification but no paid audit). Gmail/Drive are *restricted* → would trigger the annual CASA security assessment. This is a hard line.
23	
24	## Triggers (manifest)
25	
26	- `calendar.eventOpenTrigger` → first render of the visitor card (attendees from payload, no backend call).
27	- `calendar.eventUpdateTrigger` → re-render on attendee add/remove; surface the per-guest include toggle (the online/offline solution).
28	- action handlers (edit/send) → `UrlFetchApp` POST to `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/*` → re-rendered card; then write confirmation back to the event.
29	- `calendar.homepageTrigger` (Phase 3, optional) → standalone "today's visitors" panel.
30	
31	## Stack & deploy
32	
33	- **Runtime:** Google Apps Script (CardService). Google-hosted — **₹0 infra.**
34	- **Deploy:** `clasp` (Google Apps Script CLI) → Google. **Not Bifrost, not EAS.** The umbrella's "push to `personal` remote for Bifrost deploys" rule does **not** apply here.
35	- **Auth to engine:** the action handler sends the host's identity (ID token); the engine verifies it so a host can only register visits for their own tenant.
36	
37	## Status
38	
39	**Phase 1 MVP card exists** (card-first): `appsscript.json` (manifest) + `Code.js` (CardService card — roster with include toggles + phone, preview, save & send; calls the engine with `getIdentityToken()`). Engine Phase 0 is implemented + tested. To install/test locally see **`SETUP.md`** (ngrok tunnel → local engine, real OAuth token).
40	
41	Canonical plan: `../Planning-docs/2026-06-16_calendar_visitor_addon_build_plan.md`. Engine contract: `../office-ops-engine/spec/2026-06-16_calendar_addon_register_endpoint.md`. First version of `Code.js` was written outside Google — refine in the Apps Script editor (it can run/log there).
42

# Auxilio Workspace

Google **Workspace Add-on** for Auxilio (Office Ops). Lets a host register an in-person visit **from inside the Google Calendar event** — the Google-sanctioned, mobile-capable version of the "Visit tab" idea. This is a **channel surface**, not a backend: it renders cards and forwards intent to `office-ops-engine`.

> Read the umbrella `../CLAUDE.md` for the four constraints and UX rules. They apply here too.

## What this is (and isn't)

- **Is:** an Apps Script add-on (CardService) in the Calendar right rail on **web + the mobile Calendar app**. **The whole host flow lives in the card** (card-first, D5) — roster, per-guest include toggle, name/phone edits, template + location pickers, email preview, send, manage — via card form inputs + action handlers that call the engine over HTTPS.
- **Isn't:** a place for business logic. Dedup, extraction, invite/pass issuance, and lifecycle flows all live in `office-ops-engine`'s visitor plugin. This repo is a **dumb adapter** (per the umbrella architecture).
- **Isn't (v1):** a web app. We **do not** bounce the host to a browser tab — that breaks "everything in front of the user," worst on mobile. A full web editor is **deferred/optional** (D6), built only on evidence the card ceiling is real.

## Hard rules (decided 2026-06-16)

1. **Contract lives in the engine, not here.** The add-on POSTs to `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/register` with a Google ID token; the engine verifies it, normalizes to a `NormalizedCalendarEvent{ source: 'marketplace' }`, and feeds the existing spine `ingestCalendarEvent` — the same one the `.ics`/magic-address ingest calls. Dedup is on `tenantId + iCalUID` (the `calendar_event_links` ledger), so the add-on path and the room-resource path converge instead of duplicating. **Send the canonical `iCalUID`** (from the Advanced Calendar Service `Calendar.Events.get(...).iCalUID`) so convergence actually works. Full contract: `../office-ops-engine/spec/2026-06-16_calendar_addon_register_endpoint.md`.
2. **Internal-first.** Published privately to the Appointy Workspace domain (admin one-click push to all users — no per-user install). **No Google OAuth verification needed while internal.** Public Marketplace listing is deferred packaging over this same code.
3. **Two-way without broad OAuth.** Use the narrow current-event scopes:
   - `addOns.calendar.currentEventAccess = READ_WRITE`
   - `.../auth/calendar.addons.current.event.read`, `.../auth/calendar.addons.current.event.write`

   Attendees arrive in the trigger payload (no Calendar API call). `READ_WRITE` lets us write the pass link / "✓ Registered" back onto the *open* event. Broad calendar OAuth stays in the engine's ingest pipeline — **do not duplicate it here.**
4. **Calendar-only scopes. Never add Gmail or Drive.** Calendar is a *sensitive* scope (verification but no paid audit). Gmail/Drive are *restricted* → would trigger the annual CASA security assessment. This is a hard line.

## Triggers (manifest)

- `calendar.eventOpenTrigger` → first render of the visitor card (attendees from payload, no backend call).
- `calendar.eventUpdateTrigger` → re-render on attendee add/remove; surface the per-guest include toggle (the online/offline solution).
- action handlers (edit/send) → `UrlFetchApp` POST to `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/*` → re-rendered card; then write confirmation back to the event.
- `calendar.homepageTrigger` (Phase 3, optional) → standalone "today's visitors" panel.

## Stack & deploy

- **Runtime:** Google Apps Script (CardService). Google-hosted — **₹0 infra.**
- **Deploy:** `clasp` (Google Apps Script CLI) → Google. **Not Bifrost, not EAS.** The umbrella's "push to `personal` remote for Bifrost deploys" rule does **not** apply here.
- **Auth to engine:** the action handler sends the host's identity (ID token); the engine verifies it so a host can only register visits for their own tenant.

## Status

**Phase 1 MVP card exists** (card-first): `appsscript.json` (manifest) + `Code.js` (CardService card — roster with include toggles + phone, preview, save & send; calls the engine with `getIdentityToken()`). Engine Phase 0 is implemented + tested. To install/test locally see **`SETUP.md`** (ngrok tunnel → local engine, real OAuth token).

Canonical plan: `../Planning-docs/2026-06-16_calendar_visitor_addon_build_plan.md`. Engine contract: `../office-ops-engine/spec/2026-06-16_calendar_addon_register_endpoint.md`. First version of `Code.js` was written outside Google — refine in the Apps Script editor (it can run/log there).

content.content:
# office-ops-engine

The **central intelligence backend** for Office Ops Intelligence — the system that makes office operations smarter, more efficient, and future-proof. Everything operational flows through here.

> Root context: see `../CLAUDE.md` for the product vision, constraints (cost, user literacy, device capability, infrastructure), and how this subrepo fits the umbrella.
> Repo map: see `REPO_GUIDE.md` for a self-contained, code-grounded description of what's actually in this repo and how it works — written to survive removal of the umbrella/Planning docs. Read it for "what exists and how it runs"; this file is for "how to behave here."

## What this service does

- Ingests messages and events from channels (WhatsApp, Telegram, web forms, PWAs) — channels are input adapters, not the product
- Classifies them into **domains** (visitor, inventory, maintenance, kitchen, ...)
- Extracts structured data with AI agents
- Routes work to **plugins** (bounded operational systems)
- Persists, syncs to Sheets, fans out notifications, surfaces overdue items
- Serves a REST/RPC API consumed by `ops-dashboard`, `visitor-web`, and `field-app`

Keep in mind: solutions built here must stay cheap to run (cost), simple to operate (literacy), and light on bandwidth/compute (device + infrastructure constraints). See root `CLAUDE.md` for the full constraint list.

Stack: **TypeScript + Mastra + Bun + Postgres + Drizzle + Pinecone**. Node `>=22.13.0`.

## Architectural vision (must read)

This subrepo has a canonical architecture doc — **read `ARCHITECTURE.md` before any structural change**. Summary:

- `src/mastra/core/` — channel-agnostic primitives (routing, scheduling, contracts). No WhatsApp/Telegram/visitor specifics here.
- `src/mastra/channels/` — thin adapters (telegram/, whatsapp/). Translate platform events to internal messages. No business logic.
- `src/mastra/domains/` — AI classification + extraction schemas. Not a dumping ground for general logic.
- `src/mastra/plugins/` — bounded operational systems (visitor, inventory, ...). Each owns its rules, routes, jobs, internal helpers. Expose a clean entrypoint; other code depends on that, not deep internals.
- `src/mastra/agents/`, `workflows/`, `tools/`, `scorers/` — Mastra primitives. **Register new ones in `src/mastra/index.ts`.**

The non-negotiable: **a new operational area (e.g. "kitchen orders") should be a new plugin + maybe a new domain, with minimal core changes.**

## Build for the automation surface (consider, don't enforce)

Long-term vision: every domain exposes its **triggers, actions, and events** to the automation engine via `src/mastra/modules/<module>/flow-pack.ts` (see `FLOW_PACK_GUIDE.md`). `visitor`, `cafeteria`, `system` already do this; `inventory` does not yet — and that is fine for now.

You are **not** required to ship a flow-pack with every new feature. You **are** required to leave it cheap to add later:

- At meaningful state transitions (e.g. lot received, low stock, visitor checked in, request approved), have a single clear emit point with a typed payload — not logic smeared across handlers. The eventual `runFlowRules("module", "module.event", payload)` call should be a one-line drop-in.
- Side-effects that a user might plausibly automate (send email, create task, notify channel, create purchase request) should be reachable as **a single function with a typed param object**, so wrapping it as a registered action later is mechanical.
- Use stable, dotted naming for internal events even before they're registered: `inventory.low_stock`, `visitor.checked_in`. That way the eventual trigger key is already chosen.
- If a feature's shape would make later automation-surfacing painful (tightly-coupled side effects, untyped payloads, ad-hoc event names), call it out — don't silently fix it, but don't paint the code into a corner either.

Do **not** add `registerTriggers` / `registerActions` calls or create a `flow-pack.ts` unless the user asks.

## Commands

```bash
npm run dev              # Mastra Studio at localhost:4111 (long-running, separate terminal)
npm run start:backend    # Run the bun HTTP server (port 4112)
npm run bot:dev          # Same with --watch
npm run build            # Production build
npm run dev:all          # Engine + dashboard + visitor-web concurrently
```

Tunnels (for exposing local to WhatsApp webhooks etc.):

```bash
npm run backendtunnel    # ngrok the backend (4112)
npm run visitortunnel    # ngrok the visitor PWA (5174)
npm run mastratunnel     # SSH-tunnel Mastra Studio
```

## Mastra-specific guardrails

- **Before any Mastra-related code change, load the `mastra` skill.** Mastra's APIs change between minor versions; cached knowledge is unreliable. The skill reads up-to-date docs from `node_modules`.
- Use Zod (or equivalent) schemas for every tool's input/output.
- Run `npm run build` to verify compilation after non-trivial changes.
- Don't modify `node_modules` or Mastra's internal DB files.

See `AGENTS.md` for the longer version of these rules.

## Deploy — read this before running `bifrost deploy`

This service deploys to Bifrost project **`auxilio-v2`**, env `dev`, service `ops-engine`. Public URL: `https://ops-engine-dev-330299.bifrost.saastack.site`.

**Always pass `--commit <full-sha>` explicitly.** Bifrost tags images as `<project-id>/<branch>:sha-<short>` at the **project** level. When two services share branch `main`, deploying one without `--commit` can pick up the other service's last image tag. Symptom we hit: the ops-dashboard URL served the visitor-web HTML. Sanity-check after deploy:

```bash
curl -s https://ops-engine-dev-330299.bifrost.saastack.site/health
```

Bifrost is wired to the `personal` remote (`junaid-appointy/office-ops-engine`), not `origin` (`AppointyIncubator/*`). Push to `personal main` before deploying.

Env is loaded from `office-ops-engine/env.updated` (not committed).

## Never do

- Commit `.env`, `env.updated`, or any `*.json` service-account credential files (e.g. `appointy-ops-*.json`, `auxilio-*.json` — already at repo root, treat as secrets).
- Hardcode API keys — always read from env.
- Put visitor-only / inventory-only logic in `core/` or `channels/`.
- Cross-import deep internal files of one plugin from another — go through the public entrypoint.
- Deploy without `--commit`.

- `ARCHITECTURE.md` — canonical architecture & boundaries
- `AGENTS.md` — Mastra rules
- `FLOW_PACK_GUIDE.md` — module contract for exposing triggers/actions/events to the automation engine (read before adding a flow-pack)
- `Generalize_Flow_Editor.md` — flow-editor design notes
- `spec/`, `postman/` — API contracts and request collections
- `drizzle.config.ts`, `data/` — schema and seed data

22	## Vision expansion (user, 2026-06-16) — bigger than a CardService card
23	User wants Auxilio Workspace to be a full Workspace **app** (not just a calendar plugin) taking **per-user calendar OAuth, two-way (read+write)**, surfacing much of the office-ops suite over time. Visitor flow envisioned:
24	- Host creates event → we nudge if it looks like a visitor event.
25	- Fetch all guests → build an editable invite roster the host **fully customizes**: per-guest name, phone, include/exclude (the per-guest opt-out is how the online/offline-mixed-attendees case is solved), pass template, email template, visit location, etc.

30	Key design decisions reached/implied (confirm before building):
31	- **Card-first (D5, decided 2026-06-16 — REVERSED the earlier web-editor handoff):** the WHOLE host flow lives in the CardService card (roster, per-guest include toggle, name/phone, template + location pickers, email preview, send, manage) via card form inputs + action handlers. **Do NOT bounce the host to a browser tab** — breaks "everything in front of the user," worst on mobile where OpenLink ejects to a browser. The 90% flow fits CardService widgets (text inputs, switches, dropdowns, buttons, images). A full web editor is **deferred/optional (D6)**, built only on evidence the card ceiling is real. Email preview in-card = native summary + send-test-to-self for MVP; pixel-accurate HTML→image preview deferred (infra cost).
32	- **Honest limit:** Workspace add-ons CANNOT auto-pop a nudge (same platform wall as the native tab). Proactive "nudge on create" must come from the **backend calendar-watch** (already have calendar-watch.ts) via Slack/email; in-context card only shows when host opens the panel.

Draft-absent (room-resource only) → auto-create as today.
34	- **Phone not in calendar** → pre-fill from past-visitor history by email, else manual.
35	- **Scopes (D7, rev — protects existing Slack calendar OAuth):** do NOT widen the shared per-user OAuth. It stays `calendar.events.readonly` at CURRENT_SCOPE_VERSION='v2-contacts' — bumping it fires markStaleScopeConnections → reconnect-storms every connected Slack host. Write-back instead uses the add-on's `currentEventAccess = READ_WRITE` (current-event scope, granted at install). Sensitive, no CASA. Echo loop mostly defanged by draft_authoritative gating (re-ingest only retimes/noops).
36	- **Don't duplicate:** reuse visitor-web/ops-dashboard components + renderBrandedEmail (see [[project_email_branding]]) for the editor/preview; add-on deep-links in. Don't build a parallel management UI.
37	- **Forks resolved (2026-06-16):** host editing UI = **the card itself (card-first, D5)** — no web editor in v1. The deferred web editor (D6), if ever built, lives in `auxilio-workspace` (NOT visitor-web/guard PWA, NOT ops-dashboard). **v1 scope = visitor invite creation + management ONLY** — no broader suite, no "today's visitors" homepage panel (North Star, deferred).

---
name: project_calendar_visitor_addon
description: "Calendar visitor Workspace Add-on — internal-first decision, thin-surface architecture, narrow scopes."
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: cea9592c-1fe2-442e-be75-9ad630dcf0e5
---

Decided 2026-06-16: build the Google Workspace Add-on for visitor registration from the Calendar event page **Internal-first** (publish privately to Appointy domain, admin one-click push to all users → no per-user install, no Google verification, no infra). Public Marketplace listing = deferred packaging over the SAME code/engine, earns its verification cost only when a second tenant wants it.

Key build constraints to honor:
- **Add-on is a dumb surface, not a pipeline.** The engine spine ALREADY exists: `ingestCalendarEvent(NormalizedCalendarEvent)` in calendar-ingest.ts, and `CalendarEventSource` already includes `'marketplace'`. So NO new core fn (the `upsertVisitFromCalendarEvent` name was a placeholder — don't build it). Build only an authenticated producer route `POST /api/visitor/calendar/addon/register`: verify Google ID token → normalize to NormalizedCalendarEvent{source:'marketplace'} → ingestCalendarEvent → enrich passes for write-back. Dedup-for-free on tenantId+iCalUid via calendar_event_links (matches [[project_calendar_visitor_invite]]). Engine spec: office-ops-engine/spec/2026-06-16_calendar_addon_register_endpoint.md.
- **Two-way without broad OAuth:** use narrow `currentEventAccess = READ_WRITE` current-event scopes. Attendees arrive in the trigger payload (no Calendar API call); write pass link / "✓ Registered" back to the same event. Broad calendar OAuth stays in the existing ingest pipeline, NOT duplicated in the add-on.
- **No CASA:** calendar = sensitive (not restricted). Never add Gmail/Drive scopes — that flips us to restricted and triggers the paid annual audit. Hard line.
- **Runtime:** Apps Script (Google-hosted, ₹0) as thin client → HTTPS to office-ops-engine.

**Sequencing pushback the user accepted in principle:** research said build the add-on only on evidence hosts forget to add the room resource, and room-resource isn't shipped yet. Plan is designed-and-ready Phase 2 behind a room-resource pilot. Triggers: eventOpenTrigger, eventUpdateTrigger (also Meet-detection for online-meeting guard), action handler.

**Home (decided 2026-06-16):** named **"Auxilio Workspace"**, lives in its own subrepo `auxilio-workspace/` at umbrella root with its OWN git repo (sibling to visitor-web/field-app). NOT inside office-ops-engine — runs on Google's servers, deploys via `clasp` to Google Apps Script (not Bifrost/EAS), so the personal-remote/Bifrost rule does not apply. Scaffold (CLAUDE.md, README, .gitignore) created; git init'd; not yet committed.

## Vision expansion (user, 2026-06-16) — bigger than a CardService card
User wants Auxilio Workspace to be a full Workspace **app** (not just a calendar plugin) taking **per-user calendar OAuth, two-way (read+write)**, surfacing much of the office-ops suite over time. Visitor flow envisioned:
- Host creates event → we nudge if it looks like a visitor event.
- Fetch all guests → build an editable invite roster the host **fully customizes**: per-guest name, phone, include/exclude (the per-guest opt-out is how the online/offline-mixed-attendees case is solved), pass template, email template, visit location, etc.
- Show a **live preview of the invite email** before sending.
- Host manages events + invites from the app.
- Keep existing behaviors: cancel invites on event delete; cancel-old + send-new on reschedule.

Key design decisions reached/implied (confirm before building):
- **Card-first (D5, decided 2026-06-16 — REVERSED the earlier web-editor handoff):** the WHOLE host flow lives in the CardService card (roster, per-guest include toggle, name/phone, template + location pickers, email preview, send, manage) via card form inputs + action handlers. **Do NOT bounce the host to a browser tab** — breaks "everything in front of the user," worst on mobile where OpenLink ejects to a browser. The 90% flow fits CardService widgets (text inputs, switches, dropdowns, buttons, images). A full web editor is **deferred/optional (D6)**, built only on evidence the card ceiling is real. Email preview in-card = native summary + send-test-to-self for MVP; pixel-accurate HTML→image preview deferred (infra cost).
- **Honest limit:** Workspace add-ons CANNOT auto-pop a nudge (same platform wall as the native tab). Proactive "nudge on create" must come from the **backend calendar-watch** (already have calendar-watch.ts) via Slack/email; in-context card only shows when host opens the panel.
- **Visit Draft model (proposed):** a curated roster keyed on iCalUid is the **authoritative source** once a host customizes — passive .ics/OAuth paths then only mutate time (reschedule) or cancel, never regenerate from raw attendees (which would wipe customization). Draft-absent (room-resource only) → auto-create as today.
- **Phone not in calendar** → pre-fill from past-visitor history by email, else manual.
- **Scopes (D7, rev — protects existing Slack calendar OAuth):** do NOT widen the shared per-user OAuth. It stays `calendar.events.readonly` at CURRENT_SCOPE_VERSION='v2-contacts' — bumping it fires markStaleScopeConnections → reconnect-storms every connected Slack host. Write-back instead uses the add-on's `currentEventAccess = READ_WRITE` (current-event scope, granted at install). Sensitive, no CASA. Echo loop mostly defanged by draft_authoritative gating (re-ingest only retimes/noops).
- **Don't duplicate:** reuse visitor-web/ops-dashboard components + renderBrandedEmail (see [[project_email_branding]]) for the editor/preview; add-on deep-links in. Don't build a parallel management UI.
- **Forks resolved (2026-06-16):** host editing UI = **the card itself (card-first, D5)** — no web editor in v1. The deferred web editor (D6), if ever built, lives in `auxilio-workspace` (NOT visitor-web/guard PWA, NOT ops-dashboard). **v1 scope = visitor invite creation + management ONLY** — no broader suite, no "today's visitors" homepage panel (North Star, deferred).

## NO AUTO-SEND — all calendar paths, effective now (decision 2026-06-17, REVERSES earlier "auto-send stays for room-resource")
User chose: nothing auto-sends — neither the OAuth poll NOR the room-resource/.ics path — and turn it off NOW (accepting that calendar→pass is draft-only until the add-on UI+nudge ship). IMPLEMENTED in office-ops-engine: `ingestCalendarEvent` new-event + never-sent-reschedule branches now call `draftFromEvent` (build/refresh Visit Draft, status 'drafted', NO invites) instead of `createInvitesForEvent` (removed). Invites created ONLY via `/addon/send` or the draft-authoritative reschedule (moving already-sent passes). Cancel still cancels sent passes. Removed the redundant flag-gated pre-warm from calendar-sync (ingest drafts for all sources now). OAuth scope untouched (no reconnect storm). Build green; unit tests pass.
- **Anti-lag (hard constraint D10):** card-first already removes the worst source (web-app handoff). First card render payload-only ≤300ms (no backend call); **pre-warm the Visit Draft via calendar-watch** so the engine call behind the first interaction hits a warm cache (load-bearing fix — same work as the nudge); each card action = action handler → engine round-trip → re-rendered card, keep ≤~1s; optimistic send (emails/flows async after card returns); debounced preview; OAuth once. Budget: first card render ≤300ms, each action ≤~1s, send feels instant. Test on real mid Android.

## Phase 0 engine — IMPLEMENTED (2026-06-17, uncommitted)
Landed in office-ops-engine: `addon-auth.ts` (verifyAddonIdToken + isInternalHostEmail, env AUXILIO_WORKSPACE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID); `visit-drafts.ts` (Visit Draft store + materializeDraft, phone-from-history pre-fill); `visit_drafts` table + `draft_authoritative` col on calendar_event_links (postgres-schema.ts); five `/api/visitor/calendar/addon/*` routes (draft GET/PATCH, preview, send, cancel-guest) in routes.ts, ID-token authed, reusing createVisitorInvite/renderPassEmail; gated draft-authoritative branch + recordDraftMaterialization/getLinkedInvitationIds in calendar-ingest.ts. `npm run build` green; tsc clean for new/changed files (20 pre-existing errors elsewhere). Did NOT touch CURRENT_SCOPE_VERSION/shared OAuth scope. **Deferred:** pre-warm hook in calendar-watch.ts (lazy getOrCreateDraft covers correctness); regression+convergence tests (NO test harness exists in repo — no test script / no *.test.ts; needs runner decision).

## Phase 1 card MVP built (2026-06-17, untested in Google)
auxilio-workspace now has the Apps Script add-on: `appsscript.json` (Calendar add-on, eventOpen/eventUpdate triggers, currentEventAccess READ_WRITE, calendar.events.readonly + script.external_request + userinfo.email/openid scopes, urlFetchWhitelist=ngrok domain) + `Code.js` (card-first CardService: roster with include switches + phone inputs, Preview, Save&send; auth via ScriptApp.getIdentityToken(); fetches iCalUID via Advanced Calendar Service so engine dedups). Setup chosen: **ngrok tunnel → local engine** (backendtunnel fixed domain) + **real OAuth token**. Full runbook in auxilio-workspace/SETUP.md (GCP project + Internal consent screen + set engine AUXILIO_WORKSPACE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID to the token's aud; clasp create/push; Deploy→Test deployments→Install). Code.js is a first draft written outside Google — refine in the Apps Script editor. Use a DEV/throwaway DATABASE_URL (no-auto-send is live in code).

## Lifecycle + manage-screen (implemented 2026-06-17) — plan: Planning-docs/2026-06-17_addon_lifecycle_and_manage_screen_plan.md
Better-flow chosen: ONE toggle = "should have a pass" + ONE reconcile (`applyDraft`). Decisions locked: (A) Manage cancel = toggle + "Update passes" button (batched, not immediate); (B) removal auto-cancel on card-open + event-move ONLY (poll never cancels — partial-list safety). Implemented: `applyDraft` in visit-drafts (create newly-included, cancel newly-excluded, keep rest; persists status/invitationId; ledger via recordDraftMaterialization). `/addon/send` + `/addon/apply` both call applyDraft. getOrCreateDraft gained `{reconcileRemovals}` opt — card path (true) drops+cancels removed-sent guests; poll path (false, default) keeps all. Host-name fix: `resolveHostDisplayName(email, fallback)` from staff registry (token has no name claim → was showing email); used in applyDraft/materializeDraft/preview. Card: Manage mode when draft.materialized (toggle on=has pass, status labels, "Update passes"); Setup mode otherwise; onSaveAndSend re-renders via updateCard to switch to Manage after apply. Event move (passive draft_authoritative reschedule) reissues included only; delete cancels all (existing CANCEL path). Build green, 10 unit pass.
**STILL OPEN: Calendar.Events.get is unreliable** — it often returns attendees=[] even when guests exist (and sometimes 404s). So the CARD trusts `e.calendar.attendees` (payload) as the source of truth for attendees; Events.get is best-effort ONLY for iCalUID/title/times. Empty-incoming-attendees guard in getOrCreateDraft prevents mass-cancel from a transient empty list.

## Refinements (2026-06-17 batch)
- **Cancellation emails:** extracted shared `notifyInviteCancelled(invitationId,{cancelledBy,reason})` in invite-manager.ts; called from applyDraft (toggle-off), getOrCreateDraft (guest removed), and calendar-ingest CANCEL (event deleted, refactored to use it). cancelledBy must != 'visitor' for the seed flow email node to fire.
- **Empty events instant:** card short-circuits (renders "add a guest" with NO Calendar/engine call) when payload has 0 external attendees — fixes long loader.
- **Email template:** add-on uses `visitor.email.pass` key (policies.ts getVisitorUiConfig → getTemplate(key||'visitor.email.pass')) = the admin-editable default. If admin's customization doesn't show, it's because the engine points at a DIFFERENT db than the dashboard. No separate "default selection" feature exists.
- **CardService limitation:** can't grey/disable a button on live form change (no client reactivity without per-toggle round-trip). "Update passes" stays enabled with a "No changes" guard.
- **Name resolution:** added contacts.readonly + contacts.other.readonly scopes + people.googleapis.com to urlFetchWhitelist. Card resolves real contact name via People API searchContacts+otherContacts.search (needs empty-query warmup) for guests whose name is still auto-derived (cardDeriveName_ mirrors engine nameFromEmail), then PATCHes it onto the draft → one-time cost per guest. Requires re-consent (new scopes).
- Card row now shows just the email (name lives in the editable Name input).

content.text:
Both fixes are done and verified. Here's what I changed and why.

## 1. Bug: false "Pass sent" + toggle-on for an internal guest

**Root cause** (`office-ops-engine/.../visit-drafts.ts` → `adoptExistingPasses`): when building a draft, the engine tries to "adopt" a pass that already exists for a guest so it doesn't offer a duplicate. It had two matchers — an exact UID match (strong) and a **loose identity match** (`visitor_email` + event date). That loose match ran for *every* guest. Your old event's workspace guest happened to have some active invitation on that date (from an unrelated visit/test), so it got adopted onto this event → `include=true`, `status='sent'`, `materialized=true`.

**Fix:** the loose identity match now fires **only for guests who are visitors by default** (external or different-office — `guest.include === true`). A default-OFF internal colleague is no longer auto-adopted; the exact-UID match still works for everyone.

Trade-off I accepted: in the rare case where a host already made a dashboard pass for an internal colleague *and* toggles them on *and* sends, they could get a duplicate (harmless, not destructive) — far better than the common false "Pass sent" on every normal internal event.

## 2. Show that internal people are internal

Added an `internal` flag per guest (`= !includeDecider`, i.e. exactly the default-off same-workspace colleagues), set in `buildRoster` and re-derived on each open so older drafts get it too. The route already serializes the roster, so no route change.

In the add-on card (`auxilio-workspace/Code.js`) an internal guest now shows:
- a **circular building icon** (Lucide `building` on a muted grey circle, embedded as a data-URI — stays within the Lucide-only / no-emoji house rule) in place of the photo avatar;
- a **"Internal · Workspace"** top-label badge;
- status reads **"Internal — no pass needed"** (grey) instead of a bare "No pass" that looks like something's missing.

The toggle still starts off and the host can still flip it on.

## Verification
- `bun test visit-drafts.test.ts` → 6 pass (added `internal`-flag assertions).
- Added an opt-in integration regression test: an internal colleague with an unrelated same-day pass is **not** adopted (`status='pending'`, `materialized=false`, `internal=true`).
- `tsc --noEmit` clean for the touched files; `node --check Code.js` passes.

Note: `Code.js` changes ship via `clasp push` to Apps Script (not committed/deployed here per repo rules) — let me know when you want to push.

One thing worth a real-device check: the circular building data-URI icon renders fine on the mobile Calendar app (CardService icon images occasionally behave differently there). If it doesn't show, the topLabel + coloured status still carry the "internal" signal.

The new clue — **it still shows "Pass sent" every time you reopen** — points at a second, separate bug from the one I just fixed. Let me explain precisely, then fix it.

## Why it shows "Pass sent" and why it sticks

The card doesn't compute "Pass sent" live — it reads the **stored draft row** (`visit_drafts.roster`) the engine returns. Two things matter:

1. **The status is persisted.** Every code path that sets a guest to `status:'sent'` also writes it into the `visit_drafts` row. So once a guest is written as "sent", it's baked into the database for that event.

2. **The load path blindly trusts it.** When you reopen the event, `getOrCreateDraft` finds the existing row and merges with `{...prev}` — it **never re-checks whether that invitation actually exists, is active, or even belongs to this event** (`visit-drafts.ts:420-425`). So a draft that was written "sent" once shows "Pass sent" forever, even if no real pass is behind it anymore.

So your event got a `status:'sent'` written into its draft row at some earlier point (the most likely writer is the adoption logic I fixed — it grabbed an unrelated active invitation for that workspace person on that date — or a manual toggle-on+send during earlier testing). **My first fix only stops new internal events from being wrongly adopted; it does nothing for a draft that's already written.** That's why yours still shows it.

content.text:
## Answer to your "why"

You were right to push — "**it still shows on reopen**" was the tell. There are actually two distinct bugs:

**Why it showed "Pass sent" at all:** the card doesn't compute pass state live; it reads the stored `visit_drafts` row. Your event's row got a guest written with `status:'sent'` at some earlier point. The most likely writer is the adoption logic — it found an *unrelated* active invitation for that workspace person on that date (e.g. from another visit/test) and grabbed it onto this event. The event itself was never used to create a pass — it just inherited someone else's.

**Why it stays "sent" on every reopen (the real new bug):** once `status:'sent'` is in the draft row, the load path (`getOrCreateDraft`) merged it back with `{...prev}` and **never re-checked whether a real pass actually backs it**. So it's stuck forever. My first fix only stopped *new* internal events from being mis-adopted — it did nothing for a row already written, which is exactly what you saw.

## What I changed (this round)

A **self-heal on draft load**: a stored "sent" guest is reset to pending (and back to its correct default toggle) when either —
- the invitation no longer exists / isn't active, **or**
- the guest is an internal colleague whose pass isn't actually linked to *this* event (the only legit way an internal colleague is "sent" is you toggling them on + sending, which always links the pass to the event — so an unlinked internal "sent" is a phantom).

This catches your case **even if that unrelated invitation is still active elsewhere**. I also made `materialized` derive from the healed roster, so the event drops back to "Send passes" mode instead of being stuck in "Manage".

**Net effect:** the next time you open that event (after this is deployed), it self-heals — the guest flips to toggle-off, "Internal — no pass needed", building icon, and the event no longer claims a pass. No manual DB cleanup needed.

## Verified
- Pure tests pass (6/6); touched files typecheck clean.
- Added an opt-in integration test asserting a phantom "sent" guest self-heals to pending + Setup mode on reopen.

Two notes:
- These are engine changes (`visit-drafts.ts`) — they take effect once the engine is redeployed; the `Code.js` badge changes need a `clasp push`. Say the word and I'll walk through deploying.
- If you want a *definitive* post-mortem on exactly which invitation got adopted onto that event, I can run a read-only query against the dev DB to show you the draft row + the invitation it pointed at — but I'll need your OK first (per our no-ad-hoc-queries-on-the-live-DB rule).

content.text:
Don't wipe the DB — that won't fix the root cause (it'd come back, and it nukes everything else). Here's what's actually going on.

## The real root cause

Your env is fine (`GOOGLE_CALENDAR_DOMAIN=appointy.com`), so the guest *is* recognized as internal. The "Pass sent" is **stale data baked into that event's draft row**, created by a chain I'd missed:

1. An early open (before the adoption gate) grabbed an **unrelated, still-active** pass that workspace person had from some other visit that day, and wrote it onto this event's draft as `status:'sent'`.
2. Because you're the event organizer, the draft route then **wrote a `calendar_event_links` row linking that borrowed pass to this event** — making it *look* legitimately owned.
3. So my previous self-heal (which checked "is the pass active and linked here?") saw ✅ active + ✅ linked and left it alone. That's why it still showed.

The only reliable signal: an internal colleague is only *genuinely* "sent" when **you** toggle them on and send, which creates an `invited_via='calendar'` pass. A borrowed pass came from another channel (`web`/`slack`/etc.). I rewrote the self-heal to key on that, and to also unlink the stale event→pass link.

## What you need to do

**Restart the engine** that your ngrok tunnel points at (the local one — `npm run start:backend`, or `npm run bot:dev` which `--watch`es and reloads on save). My fixes are engine-side; if the running process is the old code, nothing changed — which is the most likely reason it "still shows."

Then **reopen the event once.** On that open the draft self-heals: the guest flips to toggle-off, the building/"Internal" badge appears, "Pass sent" is gone, and the bad link is removed. No DB surgery needed.

If after a confirmed restart + reopen it *still* shows "Pass sent", then my theory is wrong and we should look at the actual row — at that point I'd want your OK to run one read-only query against the dev DB to see that guest's stored `status`/`invitationId` and the invitation's `invited_via`. But try the restart first; I'm fairly confident that's it.

Verified on my end: pure tests pass, types clean, and the opt-in integration test now reproduces your exact case (active + linked + `web` pass) and asserts it heals.

input.new_string:
Confirm-card form-wipe bug (2026-06-18): pressing "Send passes" returned "No changes" + left the guest toggle off / "No pass". Cause: `onSaveAndSend` (and `onPreview`) ran on the CONFIRM card and re-called `patchFromForm_`, which maps over all guests reading `inc_/name_/phone_` form inputs — but the confirm card has none of those widgets (only the optional `email_template` dropdown). A deselected switch and a switch-absent-from-this-card BOTH arrive as missing → it can't tell them apart, so it PATCHed every guest to include=false/name=''/phone='', wiping the roster right before send. Fix (Code.js): the roster card's `onReviewSend` already persists toggles/names/phones before navigating, so the confirm-card actions must NOT re-derive them. content.text:
Fixed. This one is purely a card (Apps Script) bug — no engine involvement.

## What was happening

The send flow has two cards: the **roster card** (with the per-guest toggle/name/phone widgets) and the **confirmation card** (just the recipient list + an optional template picker).

- On the roster card, `onReviewSend` correctly reads the form and saves your toggles → the confirm card shows "1 pass will be sent." ✓
- But `onSaveAndSend` (the "Send passes" button on the **confirm** card) re-ran `patchFromForm_(iCalUid, e)` — and that function maps over every guest reading the `inc_`/`name_`/`phone_` widgets. **Those widgets don't exist on the confirm card.** A switch that's *off* and a switch that's *not on this card* both arrive identically (absent), so it can't tell them apart and wrote `include=false`, `name=''`, `phone=''` for everyone — wiping the roster the instant before sending.

Result: the send found nothing included → "No changes", and the draft was left all-excluded → toggle off / "No pass". `onPreview` on that same card had the twin bug (it also couldn't find a guest to preview).

## The fix

The roster card already persists everything before navigating, so the confirm card must **not** re-derive include/name/phone. I added `patchTemplateFromForm_` that persists *only* the email-template choice (the one widget that actually lives on the confirm card), and pointed both `onSaveAndSend` and `onPreview` at it. `onPreview` now gets its target guest from a `previewEmail` param threaded in when the confirm card is built, instead of reading switches that aren't there.

Verified: `node --check` passes; call sites confirmed (`onReviewSend` keeps the real `patchFromForm_`; the two confirm-card actions use the template-only patch).

This is an Apps Script change, so it takes effect on the next `clasp push` — not a server restart. Want me to walk through pushing it (and the internal-badge changes from earlier are in the same file, so they'll go up together)?

Confirmed the root cause. Attendees come from the payload (so the card renders fine and shows "will get a pass"), but **event times only come from `Calendar.Events.get`** — the known-flaky call. aiTitle: Fix calendar add-on guest status display

content.text:
Fixed. The "event open, add-on open, still fails" is the giveaway here.

## Why it failed

The add-on payload (`e.calendar`) gives you the **attendees** but **not the event's start/end times**. Times come *only* from `Calendar.Events.get` — the known-flaky call. For a **just-created event** it's eventually-consistent and often 404s for a second or two. So:

- Attendees arrive from the payload → the card renders fine and shows "will get a pass."
- But `Events.get` missed → the draft was saved with `start=null/end=null`.
- On send, `applyDraft` refuses to issue a pass with an unknown duration → "Could not read the event time."

And the send path never re-fetched the times, so the "reopen and try again" advice didn't reliably work — once the draft was stored time-less, it stayed that way.

## The fix (three parts)

1. **Hardened the time fetch** (`fetchEventWithRetry_`): now 4 attempts with backoff (~2.7s total) across both the event's calendar and the primary calendar — enough to ride out a new event's consistency lag.
2. **`onSaveAndSend` re-resolves the times at send** and passes `start`/`end` to the engine, so even if the draft-open missed them, the send carries fresh ones.
3. **The send route applies those times to the draft** before reconciling (and `applyDraft` persists it) — so the draft self-corrects instead of needing a manual reopen.

content.text:
Confirmed and resolved — and it was never a code bug in the send path.

## Root cause

You were sending from the **event create/edit form**, where the event isn't saved yet. The add-on shows the guest because **attendees come from the panel payload**, but the event's **date/time only exist in the Calendar API once the event is saved** — and `Calendar.Events.get` returns `Not Found` (the 404 in your diagnostic) for an unsaved event. content.text:
Confirmed it in the git history — here's the real story.

## How it "worked" before

In the earlier calendar→pass code (commit `1f2934b`), `calendar-ingest.ts` had this:

```js
const end = event.end ?? new Date(start.getTime() + 60 * 60 * 1000); // default +1h
```

So when the event's end time was missing, the engine **guessed** (start + 1 hour) and issued the pass anyway. That's why a send could go through without a fully-resolved event.

The **2026-06-17 decision** ("a pass's validity window must equal the event's real start/end — never guess") deliberately removed that fallback. The add-on's send path now goes through `resolveEventWindow`, which returns *nothing* when times are missing rather than inventing them — so it requires the event's real saved time. That change is what made the unsaved-event case start failing. It wasn't a regression by accident; it was a deliberate "don't put a wrong time on a visitor pass" call (a guard at reception checks that window).

## Can we save the event ourselves, then send?

Honestly: **no, not reliably** — and I'd push back on building it. Two hard blockers, both confirmed by today's debugging:

1. **We can't read the time the host typed into an unsaved event.** It's not in the add-on payload (that only carries attendees — that's why the roster shows but the time doesn't), and the Calendar API 404s on an unsaved event. So even if we forced a save, we'd have nothing to validate the pass window against.
2. **There's no API to "save" the compose form.** A Calendar add-on can *modify* the open event (add attendees, etc.) via `currentEventAccess`, but it can't commit the draft the user is composing — the Save button is the user's action.

So "auto-save then send" would require data we fundamentally don't have until the host saves. We'd be back to guessing the time — exactly what we removed on purpose.

## The better UX (my recommendation)

**Keep save-first — but make it a non-event.** Two reasons it's the right call:

- In real use this friction barely exists: **you always save a meeting** — nobody creates a calendar event and leaves it as an unsaved draft. You hit it only because you were testing by sending straight from the create form. The natural flow is: fill event + guests → **Save** (which you'd do anyway) → **Send passes**, and it works.
- The alternative (reintroduce a guessed window so unsaved sends "work") puts wrong times on real visitor passes. That's worse than one Save click, and it undoes a decision you made for a good reason.

So the flow I'd commit to: add guests → Save → send passes on the saved event. The banner I just shipped makes the unsaved state obvious instead of failing at the end.

**One small polish worth doing:** on an unsaved event, relabel the primary button from "Review & send" to **"Save event first"** so it's self-explanatory *before* the click, not just in the banner. Cheap, no downside.

**One alternative I considered and rejected:** show date/time pickers in the card when we can't read the event, and let the host type the visit window. It works, but it's clunky (they already set the time in the event form) and the two can drift. Not worth it.

Want me to apply the button relabel, and double-check whether the panel auto-refreshes the moment you hit Save (the `eventUpdateTrigger`) so the banner clears on its own — or leave it as the banner + guidance you have now?

EVENT_TIME_MISSING on send (2026-06-18): send failed "Could not read the event time — reopen the event and try again" even with the event + add-on open. Cause: the add-on payload (`e.calendar`) carries attendees but NOT event start/end — times come ONLY from `Calendar.Events.get`, which is flaky/eventually-consistent on a just-created event. Card change needs clasp push; engine change needs restart.

A deselected switch and a switch-absent-from-this-card BOTH arrive as missing → it can't tell them apart, so it PATCHed every guest to include=false/name=''/phone='', wiping the roster right before send. Default-off internal colleagues are no longer auto-adopted; the strong exact-UID match still adopts. Accepted trade-off: rare duplicate if a host had a dashboard pass for an internal colleague + toggles them on + sends (not destructive) — far better than the common false "Pass sent".

Persistence/self-heal (the "still shows Pass sent on every reopen" bug): `status:'sent'` is baked into the `visit_drafts.roster` JSON, and `getOrCreateDraft`'s merge (`{...prev}`) trusted it blindly — so a draft ever written 'sent' (phantom adoption, or a since-cancelled/deleted pass) showed "Pass sent" forever. CRUCIAL subtlety that defeated a first self-heal attempt: on the buggy first open the draft route (routes.ts ~656, isOrganizer+sentIds>0) ALSO wrote a calendar_event_links row LINKING the borrowed pass to this event — so "active + linked-here" checks both pass; the phantom looks legit. The reliable signal: an internal colleague is legitimately 'sent' ONLY via host toggle-on+send, which creates an `invited_via='calendar'` pass; a phantom-adopted pass came from another channel (web/slack/excel/walk-in). Self-heal in getOrCreateDraft resets a stored 'sent' guest to pending + classifier-default include when (a) `getActiveInvitationById` finds no active invite, OR (b) `g.internal && active.invited_via !== 'calendar'`; it also calls `unlinkInvitationFromCalendar(id)` to drop the stale event→pass link. `materialized` is derived from the healed roster (any sent OR cancelled), NOT sticky, so a fully-phantom draft drops back to Setup mode. Stuck events self-heal on next open — BUT the running engine (ngrok→LOCAL) must be restarted to pick up the new code; no DB wipe needed. Opt-in test reproduces the active+linked+web phantom and asserts heal + unlink.

The add-on payload carries attendees (so the roster renders), but `Calendar.Events.get` returns 404 ("Not Found") for an unsaved/not-yet-propagated event, and that's the ONLY source of start/end → no time → send fails. Confirmed via temp engine file-log (`bodyStart:null`, iCalUid=eventId fallback) + card diagnostic (`got=false … err=…Not Found`, cal=organizer's own address). NOT a scope issue (attendees prove the current-event scopes work; calendar.events.readonly was a red herring). Works fine on SAVED events. Done. Here's what's now live and the flow to expect.

## What changed
- **Unsaved event:** the panel shows the "Save the event…" banner, and the footer button now reads **"Save event first"** — clicking it just reminds you to save (instead of leading you into the confirm screen and failing at the end).
- **After you click Save:** Google fires the `eventUpdateTrigger`, the panel re-renders, the event is now readable from the API, the banner clears, and the button flips to **"Review & send"** — which works.

So the flow is: **add guest → Save the event → Send passes.** The only "extra" step is Save, which you do anyway to create the meeting.

## Please verify one thing
When you hit **Save** with the panel open, does it **auto-refresh** to "Review & send" on its own? It should (that's the `eventUpdateTrigger`), but I'm not 100% certain Google fires it on the *first* save of a brand-new event. If it doesn't refresh by itself, clicking the event again (reopening the panel) will pick up the saved state. Tell me which happens — if it doesn't auto-refresh, I'll add a small "I've saved — refresh" affordance so it's still one tap.

## On the original question
To close the loop honestly: the time being visible in the create form was a fair thing to expect we could use, but the empirical dump settled it — Google's add-on payload contains `id | attendees | conferenceData | calendarId | organizer | capabilities` and **no start/end**, so the time genuinely isn't available to us until the event is saved. We're not guessing a window (that decision was deliberate, so passes match the meeting), hence save-first.

message.content: why don't we give the button on unsaved event as : Schedule invites, and we will save the calendar event id and all the config and changes the user did in the add on page, and if they save it, we will send the invite passes as per that, and if they don't we will discard it, what do you think about this, give me better suggestion if you have.

content.text:
Good instinct — deferring instead of blocking is the right direction, and it actually respects the "never guess the time" rule (we wait for the real saved time rather than inventing one). But let me pressure-test it honestly, because the idea has two load-bearing assumptions that aren't guaranteed, and one of them we can verify *right now* with the logging I just added.

## The hard parts (where this lives or dies)

**1. Correlation — does the unsaved event's ID survive the save?**
Your plan hinges on "save the calendar event id now, match it to the saved event later." That only works if the ID Google gives us for the *unsaved* event equals the ID after it's saved. If Google mints a new ID on save (very possible for a draft being composed), the link is broken and we can't tell which saved event the scheduled config belongs to. **This is the make-or-break question, and we can answer it with the payload logging already in place:** open the panel on an unsaved event (logs id A), save it, reopen (logs id B). If A == B, your design is viable. If not, we need a different anchor.

**2. The "they saved it" signal.**
The add-on only gets told about the event *while its panel is open* (the update trigger). If the host configures invites, then closes the panel and saves later, our code never runs — so nothing sends. To fulfill after the panel closes, the only reliable trigger is the **backend calendar-watch**, which requires the host's calendar to be **OAuth-connected** (we already have that feature, for move/cancel sync). Without it, "schedule then walk away" silently does nothing.

The "discard if they don't save" part is the easy bit — just expire unsaved scheduled drafts after a TTL.

## A better-engineered version of your idea

Reuse what already exists instead of building a new mechanism:

- **"Schedule invites"** persists the config (we already create a draft on every open) and marks it *pending-on-save*.
- Fulfillment rides the **existing OAuth calendar-watch**: when the host saves, Google pushes the event to the engine, it now has the real time, we match the pending draft and send. Bonus — you get move/cancel lifecycle for free, which is already built.
- **Gate it on calendar connection.** If connected → true fire-and-forget. If not → "Schedule invites" first offers the one-tap Connect (already there), or falls back to save-first.
- **For bulletproof correlation** (sidestepping problem 1): when they tap Schedule, write a marker onto the event via the `currentEventAccess: READ_WRITE` we already hold (e.g. an extended property). This is the linchpin your idea needed, and it holds:
- The unsaved event's `id` **equals** the saved event's `id`.
- The saved `iCalUID` is just **`<id>@google.com`** — derivable from the unsaved id.

That means **correlation is solved.** We can persist the config now against that id and reliably match it to the saved event later — no fuzzy matching, no marker-writing workaround needed. With correlation no longer a risk, your idea is genuinely viable and clean. Recommended shape:

- **"Schedule invites"** persists the draft config (we already create the draft on open) and flags it `pending-on-save`.

  - **Backend calendar-watch** (if the host connected their calendar) → fires even if they closed the panel — *true* fire-and-forget, and it reuses the move/cancel lifecycle you already built.
- **Discard:** pending drafts not fulfilled within a TTL (say 24h) get cleaned up.

Cause: `adoptExistingPasses` ran the LOOSE identity match (visitor_email + event date) for ALL guests, so a default-OFF colleague inherited an unrelated same-day pass. Accepted trade-off: rare duplicate if a host had a dashboard pass for an internal colleague + toggles them on + sends (not destructive) — far better than the common false "Pass sent".

content.text:
You're right that read+write calendar OAuth exists in the system — but it's worth being precise about *where*, because it changes what's possible. There are two separate OAuth contexts:

**1. The add-on's own manifest scopes** (`auxilio-workspace/appsscript.json`):
- `calendar.events.readonly` — **read only**
- `calendar.addons.current.event.write` — write, but only to the **currently-open event while the panel is executing**

This write can't run after the panel closes and can't watch for changes. So the add-on *itself* can't power "send after they close the tab."

**2. The engine's "Connect calendar" OAuth** (`office-ops-engine/.../calendar-oauth.ts`):
- `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.events` — **read + write**, scope version `v3-events-rw`

**This** is the read+write you're thinking of. The engine stores a per-user refresh token and runs the background **calendar-watch** with it. This is exactly the piece that makes "Schedule invites → fulfill after the panel is closed" work: host saves the event → Google pushes the change → the watch ingests the saved event (real time + `iCalUID`) → we match the pending-schedule draft (by `<id>@google.com`) → send.

So your idea is **fully buildable on infrastructure that already exists** — the watch, the per-user token, the draft store, and (now confirmed) the stable event ID for correlation.

Two refinements, not blockers:
- **It only kicks in for hosts who've completed the one-time "Connect calendar" step.** The add-on offers that button today, but it's not automatic on install. So "Schedule invites" would either require/prompt that connection, or fall back to fulfilling when the host reopens the panel.
- **We don't even need the *write* half for this.** Deferred-send only *reads* the saved event's time, then issues passes in our own system — no calendar write. So even an older read-only connection is enough; write only matters for the unrelated Slack "create event on a host's calendar" path.

The one engine change of substance: the watch ingest is currently **draft-only** (the 2026-06-17 no-auto-send rule). "Schedule invites" adds a narrow, explicitly-consented exception — *if a draft is flagged pending-schedule, the ingest materializes and sends it* (then clears the flag) — rather than reversing no-auto-send broadly.

Want me to write this up as an implementation plan? I'd cover: the "Schedule invites" UI + connection prompt, the `pending-schedule` flag on the draft, the ingest-path send-on-match, ID/iCalUID correlation, TTL cleanup, and how it stays consistent with the existing lifecycle (move/cancel) and the no-auto-send posture.

Base directory for this skill: /Users/appointy/work/OfficeOperationsUmbrella/.claude/skills/implementation-planning

# Implementation Planning (for low-effort hand-off)

## Why this exists

A high-effort model reasons through ambiguity, catches edge cases, makes
architectural judgment calls, and keeps cross-file changes consistent. A
low-effort model does much less of that — when it hits a gap, it fills it with a
plausible guess and moves on. That guess is where bugs and rework come from.

The fix is not to make the *implementer* smarter. It's to leave **nothing left to
decide**. Every choice that would otherwise require fresh reasoning during the
build must already be resolved, in writing, in the plan. Done right, the
implementer is transcribing a spec, not solving a problem — and that is something
a low-effort run does reliably.

So the test for every line of the plan is: **could a careful but unimaginative
implementer follow this without making a single judgment call?** If a step
requires the implementer to decide, infer, choose, or "figure out," it is not
done — push that decision up into the plan.

## What to do first

Before writing the plan, do the expensive thinking now (this skill runs at high
effort for exactly this reason):

1. **Read the actual code.** Open the files you'll touch. Don't plan against an
   imagined codebase — plan against the real one, with real names, real types,
   real signatures.
2. **Separate the two kinds of fork.** Some choices are yours to make (which
   pattern, how to structure a function); make them and commit. Others are not
   yours — they depend on product intent, external systems, or acceptance
   criteria only the user knows. Do **not** guess these; collect them as **Open
   questions** and resolve them with the user before the plan is final. A wrong
   requirement guess is the single most expensive kind, because the implementer
   will build it faithfully.
3. **Hunt edge cases up front.** Empty inputs, nulls, concurrency, failure
   paths, large inputs, auth/permissions, partial state. Decide the handling for
   each *now*, because the implementer won't go looking.
4. **Surface invariants.** Anything that must stay true (a contract, an ordering,
   a data shape other code depends on). State it explicitly so the implementer
   doesn't break it by accident.

## Plan structure

ALWAYS produce the plan in this exact shape. Save it as a markdown file
(e.g. `PLAN.md`) so it persists in context during the build. If the plan is long
or the build spans several sessions, tell the implementer to re-read this file at
the start of each session so it never works from a faded memory of it.

```markdown
# Plan: [task name]

## Goal
[2-3 sentences: what we're building and what "done" looks like. Concrete and testable.]

## Open questions (resolve before building)
[Anything that depends on the user's intent or external facts you couldn't settle
yourself. If this section is non-empty, the plan is NOT ready to hand off —
get answers first, then fold them into Decisions and delete them here.]

## Decisions already made
[Every resolved choice — library, pattern, data shape, naming. Each line removes
one judgment call from the implementer.]

## Invariants (do not break)
[Things that must stay true. The implementer should treat these as hard constraints.]

## Ground rules for the implementer
- Follow the steps in order. Do exactly what's specified — no more.
- If you hit anything this plan does not explicitly cover — an undecided choice,
  an unexpected error, or a mismatch between the plan and the actual code — STOP
  and ask rather than guessing. A question is cheaper than a wrong guess built
  faithfully.
- Don't add features, abstractions, or "improvements" beyond the plan (see Out of scope).

## Files to change
[The map: for each file, exact path + one line on what changes. The Steps below
are the actions; this is just so the implementer sees the whole footprint first.]

## Steps
[Numbered, ordered, independently verifiable. For larger work, group steps under
### Phase headings, where each phase ends in a working, verifiable state. See
"Step format" below.]

## Definition of done
[The whole-plan acceptance check: the concrete commands/tests that must pass and
behaviors that must hold for the task to be complete. The implementer runs this
last to confirm the work is actually finished, not just that each step ran.]

## Out of scope
[Explicitly list what NOT to do, so the implementer doesn't gold-plate.]
```

## Step format

Each step is the unit of work the implementer executes. A step is ready only
when it is **literal, isolated, and verifiable**:

- **Literal** — names the exact file, the exact function/class, and (where it
  matters) the exact signature, type, or return shape. Prefer "add
  `parseConfig(raw: string): Config` to `src/config.ts`" over "add config
  parsing." If a snippet removes ambiguity, include the snippet. Anchor edits to
  stable landmarks (a function name, a specific call, a unique string) rather
  than line numbers — line numbers go stale the moment the file is touched.
- **Isolated** — completable on its own and ordered so dependencies come first.
  No step should require information that only appears in a later step.
- **Verifiable** — ends with how to confirm it worked: a command to run, a test
  to add and pass, expected output, or what to check by hand. The implementer
  should never have to *decide* whether a step succeeded.

**Example of a weak step (forces reasoning):**
> 3. Handle errors in the upload flow.

**Example of a ready step (no reasoning needed):**
> 3. In `src/upload.ts`, find the `await s3.put(...)` call inside `uploadFile()`
>    and wrap it in try/catch. On error, log `upload.failed` with the object key
>    and return `{ ok: false, error: 'upload_failed' }` — do **not** throw,
>    because the caller `handleRequest()` in `handler.ts` already branches on
>    `result.ok`. Verify: run `npm test upload.test.ts`; the new
>    "rejects on S3 failure" case should pass.

The example is illustrative — apply the same literal/isolated/verifiable bar in
whatever language and stack the project actually uses.

## Edge cases and failure paths

Don't bury these inside prose steps where a low-effort run will skim past them.
Give edge cases their own explicit treatment — either a dedicated step or a
clearly labeled sub-list under the relevant step — each paired with the exact
intended behavior. If an edge case is intentionally ignored, say so in
**Out of scope** so the implementer doesn't "helpfully" handle it and add
surface area.

## Self-check before finishing

Before handing the plan off, reread it as if you were a literal-minded
implementer with no context and no inclination to think hard. For each step ask:

- Is there any place I'd have to guess a name, type, value, or path? → Specify it.
- Is there a decision phrased as a choice ("either… or", "consider", "as
  appropriate")? → Resolve it, or move it to Open questions if it's the user's call.
- Are there unresolved Open questions? → The plan isn't ready; get answers first.
- Could I tell whether each step succeeded without judgment? → Add verification.
- Does the plan as a whole have a Definition of done I can run at the end? → Add it.
- Are the steps in an order where nothing depends on a later step? → Reorder.
- Did I say what *not* to do? → Add it to Out of scope.

If any answer is "no," fix the plan rather than leaving it for the build phase.
That's the entire point: spend the reasoning here, once, at high effort, so the
implementation run never has to.

## After the plan

Tell the user they can now drop to low or medium effort for the build, and that
the plan file should stay in context (or be re-referenced) so the implementer
works from it directly. Note honestly that if implementation surfaces something
the plan genuinely didn't anticipate, the implementer should stop and ask — and
that specific step is worth bumping back to high effort. The plan reduces fresh
reasoning; it doesn't always eliminate it.

options.description: Send silently. Guests get their pass emails; the host sees the result only if they reopen the add-on panel. Simplest, least code.

content.text:
Good — that's exactly the right thing to poke at before I write the plan, because it exposes what this feature can and can't do. Let me be precise about it.

## The hard truth about Calendar add-ons

The add-on (Apps Script) **only runs while its panel is open and the user interacts**. It cannot run on its own. So if the host creates an event and never opens Auxilio — or closes before the card finishes loading — the add-on does nothing. That's a platform wall, not something we can code around.

The *only* thing that can act without the panel is the **backend calendar-watch** (the OAuth connection). That's why it's central to everything here.

## What actually happens in each case

| Host action | What happens |
|---|---|
| Opens panel, taps **Schedule invites**, then closes (even before saving) | **Works.** The intent is persisted **server-side the instant they tap Schedule** — the panel doesn't need to stay open. The watch fulfills on save. |
| Opens panel, card loads, but closes without tapping anything | A draft exists server-side (the card's load call creates it), but **no send** — they never expressed intent. |
| Never opens the panel / closes before the card loads | The add-on did nothing. **If their calendar is connected, the watch still builds a draft on save** — but it won't send (no-auto-send). |

So "Schedule invites" **does** survive "configure then close" — the tap is the commit, and it's stored immediately. What it can't survive is the host **never tapping anything at all**.

## The part that's unavoidable (and correct)

The host has to opt in **once per meeting** — open the panel and tap Schedule/Send. We *cannot* remove that, and we shouldn't: the alternative is auto-sending visitor passes to every external guest of every meeting, which is spam and is exactly the behavior we deliberately turned off on 2026-06-17. The system can't guess which meetings warrant a physical visitor pass; only the host knows.

## The real fix for "host never opened the panel"

That case isn't solved by the add-on at all — it's solved by a **proactive nudge** from the backend watch: when a connected host saves a meeting with an external guest, the engine sends them a Slack/email message — *"Meeting with an outside guest — send a visitor pass? [Open]"* — with a link back into the flow. That reaches hosts who'd otherwise never open the panel, without auto-sending.

So the robust picture is two complementary pieces, both riding the watch:
1. **Schedule invites** — for hosts who *do* open the panel: one tap, fulfilled on save.
2. **Proactive nudge** — for hosts who *don't*: the watch pings them with a link.

## Before I write the plan

Given this, do you want the plan to cover **just "Schedule invites,"** or **"Schedule invites" + the proactive nudge** as one coherent watch-based feature? My recommendation: include the nudge — it's the actual answer to the gap you just identified, and it reuses the same watch + draft plumbing. Either way, both depend on the host having connected their calendar, which is the one prerequisite worth making prominent in the UX.

3	Google **Chrome extension (MV3)** for Auxilio (Office Ops) — the **desktop** surface for registering office visitors directly inside Google Calendar. It is the richer counterpart to the `auxilio-workspace` Calendar add-on: where the add-on is mobile-capable but lives under the CardService "card ceiling," the extension gives a full React UI (realtime guest updates, per-guest invite toggle, template preview, inline edits, intentional auto-nudge).
4	
5	> Read the umbrella `../CLAUDE.md` for the four constraints and UX rules — they apply here.

9	## What this is (and isn't)
10	
11	- **Is:** an MV3 extension (WXT + React 19 + TS) that injects a trigger into Google Calendar (web) and renders the registration flow in the **side panel**. A **channel surface / dumb adapter** — it forwards intent to `office-ops-engine`.
12	- **Isn't:** a place for business logic. Dedup, draft curation, invite/pass issuance, and lifecycle all live in `office-ops-engine`'s visitor plugin. This repo renders UI and calls the engine.
13	- **Isn't:** mobile. Extensions are desktop-Chrome only. The `auxilio-workspace` add-on remains the mobile surface. Both key off the same **magic address**.
14	
15	## Architecture — three MV3 surfaces
16	
17	- **`entrypoints/background.ts`** (service worker) — the only holder of tokens + network. Mints/caches the Google `id_token` (engine auth) + `access_token` (Calendar read), runs the Calendar API client and the engine `/addon/*` client, keeps per-tab event context, routes messages.
18	- **`entrypoints/calendar.content.ts`** (content script @ `calendar.google.com`) — a **dumb trigger/observer**: detect open event, decode `eid`, inject the "Register a visitor" button (Phase 1) and the magic-address banner (Phase 2). No tokens, no business logic.
19	- **`entrypoints/sidepanel/`** (the app) — React + TanStack Query UI. Pure presentation + server state.
20	
21	```
22	content script ──msg──> background ──HTTPS(id_token)──> office-ops-engine /api/visitor/calendar/addon/*
23	side panel    ──msg──> background ──Calendar API(access_token)──> canonical iCalUID + attendees
24	```
25	
26	## Hard rules
27	
28	1. **Calendar-only scopes. Never add Gmail/Drive.** Calendar is *sensitive* (verification, no paid audit). Gmail/Drive are *restricted* → annual CASA assessment. Hard line, same as the add-on.
29	2. **Engine owns the contract.** The extension calls the existing `/api/visitor/calendar/addon/{draft,preview,send,cancel-guest}` routes (ID-token authed). Do **not** add engine signature/scope changes from here. The only engine-side change is env: add this extension's OAuth client id to `AUXILIO_WORKSPACE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` (audience allow-list).
30	3. **Background is the only secret/network holder.** Content script stays a dumb trigger; side panel stays presentation. Keep the thin-adapter boundary.
31	4. **Send the canonical `iCalUID`** (from the Calendar API `events.get`, not synthesized) so the extension path converges on the same `calendar_event_links` row as `.ics`/OAuth/add-on.
32	5. **Design: Material 3 tokens, not its code.** Our own `design/tokens.ts` + `global.css` (primary `#92288E`). No MUI/Tailwind/paper. **Icons: lucide-react only**, no Unicode glyphs/emoji in chrome.

# auxilio-extension

Google **Chrome extension (MV3)** for Auxilio (Office Ops) — the **desktop** surface for registering office visitors directly inside Google Calendar. It is the richer counterpart to the `auxilio-workspace` Calendar add-on: where the add-on is mobile-capable but lives under the CardService "card ceiling," the extension gives a full React UI (realtime guest updates, per-guest invite toggle, template preview, inline edits, intentional auto-nudge).

> Read the umbrella `../CLAUDE.md` for the four constraints and UX rules — they apply here.
> Strategy + approval analysis: `../Planning-docs/2026-06-18_calendar_visitor_chrome_extension_decision.md`.
> Build plan: the approved plan that scaffolded this repo.

## What this is (and isn't)

- **Is:** an MV3 extension (WXT + React 19 + TS) that injects a trigger into Google Calendar (web) and renders the registration flow in the **side panel**. A **channel surface / dumb adapter** — it forwards intent to `office-ops-engine`.
- **Isn't:** a place for business logic. Dedup, draft curation, invite/pass issuance, and lifecycle all live in `office-ops-engine`'s visitor plugin. This repo renders UI and calls the engine.
- **Isn't:** mobile. Extensions are desktop-Chrome only. The `auxilio-workspace` add-on remains the mobile surface. Both key off the same **magic address**.

## Architecture — three MV3 surfaces

- **`entrypoints/background.ts`** (service worker) — the only holder of tokens + network. Mints/caches the Google `id_token` (engine auth) + `access_token` (Calendar read), runs the Calendar API client and the engine `/addon/*` client, keeps per-tab event context, routes messages.
- **`entrypoints/calendar.content.ts`** (content script @ `calendar.google.com`) — a **dumb trigger/observer**: detect open event, decode `eid`, inject the "Register a visitor" button (Phase 1) and the magic-address banner (Phase 2). No tokens, no business logic.
- **`entrypoints/sidepanel/`** (the app) — React + TanStack Query UI. Pure presentation + server state.

```
content script ──msg──> background ──HTTPS(id_token)──> office-ops-engine /api/visitor/calendar/addon/*
side panel    ──msg──> background ──Calendar API(access_token)──> canonical iCalUID + attendees
```

## Hard rules

1. **Calendar-only scopes. Never add Gmail/Drive.** Calendar is *sensitive* (verification, no paid audit). Gmail/Drive are *restricted* → annual CASA assessment. Hard line, same as the add-on.
2. **Engine owns the contract.** The extension calls the existing `/api/visitor/calendar/addon/{draft,preview,send,cancel-guest}` routes (ID-token authed). Do **not** add engine signature/scope changes from here. The only engine-side change is env: add this extension's OAuth client id to `AUXILIO_WORKSPACE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` (audience allow-list).
3. **Background is the only secret/network holder.** Content script stays a dumb trigger; side panel stays presentation. Keep the thin-adapter boundary.
4. **Send the canonical `iCalUID`** (from the Calendar API `events.get`, not synthesized) so the extension path converges on the same `calendar_event_links` row as `.ics`/OAuth/add-on.
5. **Design: Material 3 tokens, not its code.** Our own `design/tokens.ts` + `global.css` (primary `#92288E`). No MUI/Tailwind/paper. **Icons: lucide-react only**, no Unicode glyphs/emoji in chrome.
6. **Animations subtle, only where needed** — CSS transitions driven by motion tokens; respect `prefers-reduced-motion`.
7. **Internal-first.** Force-installed/unlisted to our Workspace → no OAuth verification yet. Public/commercial = a later packaging + verification exercise (CWS review + OAuth verification), per the decision doc.

## Stack & commands

WXT (MV3 over Vite) + React 19 + TypeScript + TanStack Query (server state) + Zustand (UI/session/event-context) + lucide-react.

```bash
npm install
npm run dev        # WXT dev server; load .output/chrome-mv3 in chrome://extensions (Developer mode → Load unpacked)
npm run build      # production build → .output/chrome-mv3
npm run compile    # tsc --noEmit (type-check)
npm run zip        # packaged zip for store upload
```

Env: copy `.env.example` → `.env` (engine URL, OAuth client id, magic address). `WXT_` vars are inlined at build.

## Deploy / distribution

**Not Bifrost, not EAS.** Internal-first via Chrome admin **force-install** (unlisted, by extension id) or "Load unpacked" for dev. Public later via the Chrome Web Store (payments handled by our own engine; CWS no longer bills).

aiTitle: Fix calendar add-on guest status display

content.text: This is the extension — and the good news is the concern you're raising is already largely handled by a layer you might not have clocked: the extension doesn't depend on the in-page banner appearing. Let me verify the detection criteria before I claim that, though:

Confirmed how it works — and the short answer is: the in-page banner is only the *fast* path, not the only one. The extension has a durable, tab-independent layer that already covers most of what you're worried about.

## What catches the event even after you close the tab

`background.ts` runs a **`chrome.alarms` sync every 1 minute** (`background.ts:37`), plus on Chrome startup (`onStartup → doSync`). That sync (`calendar-sync.ts`) lists your saved calendar events via the Calendar API and flags the visitor (magic-address) ones. For each newly-found one it fires an **OS notification** (`notifyVisitorEvent`, `background.ts:127`) and a **toolbar badge** — *with no Calendar tab open at all*.

So: create event → add the magic address → save → close the tab → within ~1 minute you still get a desktop notification "Visitor event — open Auxilio to register passes." The in-page banner (`syncBurst` at 0/3/8s) is just the seconds-fast version when the tab stays open.

## The precise cases, honestly

| Scenario | Caught? |
|---|---|
| Create + **save** + close tab fast | ✅ Background alarm → OS notification (~1 min), or instantly if the tab's still open |
| Create but **don't save**, then close | ❌ — but there's *nothing to catch*: an unsaved event doesn't exist server-side or in the API. Correct behavior. |
| Create + save, but **no magic address** on it | ⚠️ Background sync keys on the magic address (`isMarked`), so it won't nudge. Only the explicit "Manage Visitors" button covers those — and that needs the tab open. |
| Chrome fully closed | ⏸ No detection until Chrome restarts (`onStartup` then catches up) |

## The real backstop you may be forgetting

The magic address is a **real mailbox the engine monitors**. If it's a guest, Google emails the `.ics` to it, and the engine's existing ingest builds the draft **server-side — independent of Chrome, the extension, or any tab**. That's the most durable layer, and it's where a Slack/email nudge to the host would live. So even "laptop asleep, extension uninstalled" is covered at the engine.

## So the honest verdict

Your worry holds only in one shape: a **saved, external-guest event with *no* magic address**, where the user never opens the extension. Everything else is already caught by the alarm + OS notification, or the engine's magic-address ingest.

Two ways to tighten that one gap, if you want:
1. **Make the background sync flag any external-guest event** (not just magic-address) — catches everything, but risks over-nudging on every meeting with an outsider.
2. **Lean on the engine watch** (the "Schedule/auto-fulfill on save" idea) so a host who's connected gets caught server-side regardless of the magic address.

Which gap do you actually want to close — the no-magic-address case, the latency, or the truly-hands-off auto-send? That tells me whether the next plan targets the extension's detection or the engine's fulfillment.
